title: Recurring Incident Detection
spl:
index=itsm sourcetype="snow:incident" earliest=-30d
| eval key=coalesce(cmdb_ci, category."|".subcategory)
| bin _time span=24h
| stats count by _time, key, short_description
| where count >= 3
| sort -count
description: Clusters of similar incidents within a short window signal candidates for problem records, known-error articles, or permanent fixes.
value: Clusters of similar incidents within a short window signal candidates for problem records, known-error articles, or permanent fixes.
implementation: Group by CI and/or category hash; use `cluster` or `anomalydetection` for text similarity if needed. Alert when ‚Č•N incidents per day on same key. Feed to problem management queue.
visualization: Table (recurring clusters), Bar chart (count by key), Timeline (spikes).
knownFalsePositives: Reopens and repeats that share one flaky root cause, and tickets that only look the same in the short text field; problem review still helps sort them.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
‚ÄĘ Install and configure the required add-on or app: `Splunk_TA_snow`.
‚ÄĘ Ensure the following data sources are available: Incidents (`sourcetype=snow:incident`).
‚ÄĘ For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Configure data collection
Group by CI and/or category hash; use `cluster` or `anomalydetection` for text similarity if needed. Alert when ‚Č•N incidents per day on same key. Feed to problem management queue.

Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=itsm sourcetype="snow:incident" earliest=-30d
| eval key=coalesce(cmdb_ci, category."|".subcategory)
| bin _time span=24h
| stats count by _time, key, short_description
| where count >= 3
| sort -count
```

Understanding this SPL

**Recurring Incident Detection** ‚ÄĒ Clusters of similar incidents within a short window signal candidates for problem records, known-error articles, or permanent fixes.

Documented **Data sources**: Incidents (`sourcetype=snow:incident`). **App/TA** (typical add-on context): `Splunk_TA_snow`. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed‚ÄĒrename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: itsm; **sourcetype**: snow:incident. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

‚ÄĘ Scopes the data: index=itsm, sourcetype="snow:incident", time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
‚ÄĘ `eval` defines or adjusts **key** ‚ÄĒ often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
‚ÄĘ Discretizes time or numeric ranges with `bin`/`bucket`.
‚ÄĘ `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by _time, key, short_description** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
‚ÄĘ Filters the current rows with `where count >= 3` ‚ÄĒ typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
‚ÄĘ Orders rows with `sort` ‚ÄĒ combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.


Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Validate
Compare with the ServiceNow configuration item or related CMDB view. Spot-check a few CIs, owners, and relationship fields, then confirm discovery dates if we measure freshness.

Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (recurring clusters), Bar chart (count by key), Timeline (spikes).
grandmaExplanation: We spot the same kind of break showing up again and again, so we can hand it to problem work instead of one-off firefighting.
